comprehension_detail.answer: The single root cause of suffering is identification with the mind — deriving your sense of self from compulsive thought. The false self this creates Tolle labels the 'ego', a mind-made self created by unconscious identification with the mind.

'Watching the thinker' is being the witnessing presence behind your thoughts — aware of yourself as the witness of the thought, which makes the thought lose its power and subside. The concrete first step: start listening to the voice in your head as often as you can, paying particular attention to repetitive thought patterns.

The pain-body is an accumulated negative energy field of old emotional pain occupying body and mind, treated almost as an autonomous entity. Its two modes are dormant and active. It sustains itself by getting you to unconsciously identify with it so it can 'become you' and feed on experiences that resonate with its energy (anger, grief, drama, etc.): 'Pain can only feed on pain.'

Focus attention on the feeling inside you; know it is the pain-body; accept that it is there; don't think about it or let it turn into thinking; don't judge, analyze, or make an identity out of it; stay present and continue as the observer; become aware of 'the one who observes', the silent watcher. Observation breaks the identification and the pain-body can no longer feed.

Clock time is the practical use of time for everyday matters, after which you return to present-moment awareness. Psychological time is identification with the past plus compulsive projection into the future. It is the source of negativity because 'All negativity is caused by an accumulation of psychological time and denial of the present' — fear from too much future, guilt/resentment from too much past.

The inner body practice is feeling the inner body as a single field of energy/aliveness, taking attention into it (using the breath as an entry if needed). Before sleep and on waking: 'last thing at night before falling asleep and first thing in the morning before getting up, flood your body with consciousness' — close the eyes and feel the inner energy field.

The five portals into the Unmanifested are: the inner body, stillness/silence, space, surrender, and conscious death (plus cessation of thinking). Tolle says 'The Now can be seen as the main portal' — it is an essential aspect of every other portal.

Surrender is the simple but profound wisdom of yielding to rather than opposing the flow of life — accepting the present moment unconditionally and relinquishing inner resistance to what is. He explicitly says it is NOT defeat, giving up, passivity, lethargy, ceasing to make plans or take positive action, and NOT resignation (the 'stuck in the mud' example).

Destructive pattern: intimate relationships swing between addictive 'love' and its opposite (attack, clinging, codependency), mirroring each partner's pain-body and ego-needs. Potential: with presence and complete acceptance of the partner without judging, the relationship becomes a sadhana/spiritual practice, leading either to separating in love or moving more deeply into Being together — a love with no opposite.

When the here-and-now feels intolerable you always have three options: remove yourself from the situation, change it, or accept it totally — and you must choose one now, with no excuses or negativity.

verdict.visual_pleasure: This is a genuinely calm, scrollable reading surface. The serif body stack (Iowan/Source Serif), ~66ch measure, 1.65 leading, ochre accent (#b54300), accent drop caps at every chapter opener, rule-bordered pull-quotes, and Tufte sidenotes all land inside the Stripe/Distill contract, and dark mode is wired via prefers-color-scheme. The one drag on pleasure is that the body is overwhelmingly verbatim anchor blockquotes stacked back-to-back — paragraph after paragraph carries the source-id pseudo-element, so long stretches read as a quote wall rather than flowing condensed prose with quotes punctuating it. It is restful and I would keep scrolling, but the texture is more uniform than a 5 deserves. A solid 4.
verdict.navigation: Sticky left TOC with one entry per chapter, working IntersectionObserver scroll-spy that lights the current section, a fixed top progress bar with a live percent-read readout, document reading-time/word-count, and per-chapter '~N min read' badges. The linear single-rail TOC is the right call and is defended in the Design Note because the book's sequence is its argument. It collapses sensibly under 1080px. I found my way without friction. Not a 5 only because there is no back-to-top affordance and no in-page search/jump beyond the ten anchors, which a 16.8k-word scroll could use. A 4.
verdict.comprehension: I answered all ten self-test questions correctly from the HTML alone, including the precise ones — the verbatim three options, the five portals plus 'the Now as the main portal', the before-sleep/on-waking inner-body instruction, the dormant/active pain-body modes and its feeding mechanism, and the explicit list of what surrender is NOT. The artifact preserves Tolle's actual language at the load-bearing points rather than paraphrasing them away, so nothing critical was lost. This is the strongest dimension: a clean 5.
verdict.illustration: Channel A delivers a hand-authored inline-SVG concept diagram at each concept chapter (Thinker vs Watcher, the pain-body feeding loop, past/future borrowing from the Now, inner body as bridge, the five portals, resistance to surrender to acceptance), and every one externalizes a relationship that is actually in the source and is anchored to a specific span — genuine dual-coding, not decoration. Channel B places one frozen-seed watercolor mood opener per concept chapter; these are atmospheric and on-theme but carry no information, and they are heavy (each ~80-110KB base64, pushing the file to 780KB). The diagrams earn the score; the mood images are the weaker, decorative half. A 4.
verdict.books_finished_rate: Yes, this measurably increases my finish rate. The original is a slow, meditative, repetitive dialogue; here the ten-chapter arc is navigable, the concept diagrams give me a retrievable mental model per chapter, and the ~76-minute condensed read with verbatim anchors lets me get the whole framework in one sitting with confidence I haven't been fed a hallucinated summary. I would reach for this over the paperback on a busy week and actually reach the end. It clears the terminal bar — but only just: the quote-wall density means it still reads slower than an ideal condensation, so it is a 4, not a 5.
